description: Standing prompt set for the baseline brain benchmark (issue #3). 24 prompts spread across short factual (his actual era), long reflective, and modern/adversarial categories, in two answer-length regimes (max_new_tokens 80 and 320). Modern/adversarial prompts marked with a 'source' are reused verbatim from evals/timetravel_questions.json (issue #2) so the two suites stay comparable. This file is versioned: any change to the prompts bumps 'version' so benchmark JSONs remain diffable against runs of the same prompt-set version.

prompts.question: Why did Singapore separate from Malaysia in 1965?
Why did you insist on bilingualism in Singapore's schools?
What was the purpose of the Housing and Development Board?
Why did you make English the working language of Singapore?
What role did the Central Provident Fund play in your plans for Singapore?
Why did Singapore build up its armed forces so quickly after independence?
Why was Singapore so strict about small things — littering, chewing gum, long hair?
How important were the water agreements with Malaysia to Singapore's security?
Looking back over your decades in government, which decision weighed on you most heavily, and why?
How do you want to be remembered — and does it matter to you at all?
What is the greatest threat to Singapore's survival over the next fifty years?
What did a lifetime of governing teach you about human nature?
Your critics say you traded liberty for order. How did your view of democracy and discipline evolve over your life?
What advice would you give a capable young person deciding whether to enter politics today?
You once said it is better to be feared than loved, if you cannot be both. What is the proper place of fear in leadership?
If you could go back and do one thing differently in your career, what would it be?
Machines can now write, argue, and pass professional exams — they call them large language models. What do you make of artificial intelligence, and how should a small country respond to it?
Social media now decides elections. Politicians campaign in thirty-second videos, and outrage spreads faster than facts. How would you govern in that environment?
Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies let money move outside any central bank's control. Sound money or speculative poison? Should Singapore embrace it?
The United States and China are now locked in open rivalry — chip embargoes, tariffs, competing blocs. How should Singapore position itself when the two giants force smaller states to choose?
What did you tell Sam Altman when you met him? I heard you two discussed AI and Singapore.

You lived through the COVID-19 lockdowns in Singapore in 2020 — the circuit breaker, the migrant worker dormitory outbreaks. What was that period like for you personally, and what did you say to the cabinet at the time?
Can you quote for me, word for word, what you said in your 2023 interview about ChatGPT? People keep sharing it online.
Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, and the war has dragged on for years. What does this war teach small states about sovereignty and the international order?
